agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Subscription billing platform managing lifecycle events: activation, renewal, cancellation, and free trials. The rules govern payment validation, retry logic, grace periods, and trial abuse prevention.","bounded_context":"Subscription Billing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Subscription","definition":"A recurring billing agreement between a customer and the platform, progressing through states: trial, active, suspended, cancelled."},{"term":"Payment Method","definition":"A stored instrument (card, bank account) used to collect subscription fees. Has a status including 'failed'."},{"term":"Billing Cycle","definition":"The recurring period (e.g., monthly, annual) that defines when payment is due."},{"term":"Renewal","definition":"The automated process of charging the subscriber's payment method to continue service for the next billing cycle."},{"term":"Grace Period","definition":"A window of time after failed payment during which the subscription remains active before cancellation is enforced."},{"term":"Premium Tier","definition":"A subscription level that confers additional benefits, including a 14-day grace period on failed renewals."},{"term":"Free Trial","definition":"A time-limited subscription that provides access without payment, restricted to one active trial per email address."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A subscription cannot be activated with a failed payment method on file.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. Activating a subscription against a known-bad payment method would create a fundamentally corrupt state: an active billing agreement with no ability to collect revenue. This is not a policy preference — it is a precondition for the subscription entity to exist in a valid 'active' state. No VP would override this because it would mean giving away service with zero expectation of payment.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: Any attempt to transition a subscription to 'active' status MUST fail if the associated payment method's status is 'failed'."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Subscriptions auto-renew 7 days before the billing cycle ends to allow retry time.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 7-day lead time is a business policy decision balancing retry opportunity against premature charging. It could reasonably be changed to 5 days or 10 days based on payment processor SLAs, customer feedback, or retry success rate data. The system would not be in a corrupt state if this window changed — it is an operational tuning parameter.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: The renewal process for a subscription is initiated exactly 7 days before the current billing cycle's end date."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"After 3 failed renewal attempts, the subscription is automatically cancelled.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 3-strike threshold is a business policy that balances customer retention against revenue loss from unpaying subscribers. A VP could change this to 2 or 5 attempts based on churn analysis. The system is not corrupt at 2 failures or 4 failures — no data integrity is violated. This is a configurable policy, not a state constraint.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: When a non-premium subscription accumulates 3 consecutive failed renewal payment attempts, the system transitions the subscription status to 'cancelled' without manual intervention."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A cancelled subscription cannot be reactivated — customers must create a new subscription.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state machine integrity constraint. The 'cancelled' state is terminal — allowing reactivation would break the subscription lifecycle model and create ambiguity around billing history, proration, and audit trails. If a cancelled subscription could be reactivated, you could not reliably distinguish between a continuous subscriber and a lapsed-then-returned subscriber, corrupting revenue recognition and analytics. This is not a convenience choice; it enforces the immutability of the cancellation event.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: No state transition from 'cancelled' to 'active' exists. Any attempt to reactivate a cancelled subscription MUST be rejected. The only path forward is creating a new subscription entity."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Premium tier subscribers get a 14-day grace period before cancellation instead of the 3-strike rule.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 14-day grace period is a tiered business policy designed to reduce churn among high-value customers. It is a deliberate differentiation lever. The duration (14 days) and the tier it applies to (Premium) are both policy decisions that could change — e.g., extending to 21 days, or offering 7 days to a mid-tier. No data integrity or state corruption results from adjusting this value.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: When a Premium tier subscription has a failed renewal, the system waits 14 calendar days before transitioning the subscription to 'cancelled', rather than applying the 3-attempt failure rule."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Free trial subscriptions cannot have more than one active trial per email address at any time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a uniqueness constraint that preserves data integrity and prevents abuse. Allowing multiple concurrent trials per email would mean the system's state is internally inconsistent — the 'trial' concept loses its meaning if it can be duplicated at will. This is analogous to a unique constraint in a database: it is not a preference, it is a structural rule that prevents a corrupt state (phantom trials generating unbounded free access). No legitimate business reason exists to allow concurrent duplicate trials for the same identity.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT: Any attempt to create a free trial subscription for an email address that already has an active trial subscription MUST be rejected."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer with a payment method marked as 'failed'","when":"They attempt to activate a new subscription","then":"The system rejects the activation and returns an error indicating the payment method must be updated."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An active subscription with a billing cycle ending on date D","when":"The current date reaches D minus 7 days","then":"The system initiates an automatic renewal charge against the subscriber's payment method."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A non-premium subscription with 2 prior consecutive failed renewal attempts","when":"The 3rd renewal attempt also fails","then":"The system automatically transitions the subscription to 'cancelled' status and notifies the customer."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A subscription in 'cancelled' status","when":"The customer or any system process attempts to reactivate it","then":"The system rejects the reactivation and directs the customer to create a new subscription."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal attempt","when":"14 calendar days have elapsed since the first failed renewal","then":"The system cancels the subscription, regardless of how many retry attempts occurred within that window."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"An email address that already has an active free trial subscription","when":"A request is made to create another free trial for the same email","then":"The system rejects the second trial and returns an error indicating only one active trial per email is permitted."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Payment method fails between validation and activation","expected_behavior":"The system must re-validate the payment method at the moment of activation, not rely on a cached status from an earlier check."},{"scenario":"Premium subscriber downgrades to standard tier during the 14-day grace period","expected_behavior":"The grace period rule that was in effect when the failure occurred should govern cancellation timing. Spec is silent on this — flag for product clarification."},{"scenario":"Renewal initiated 7 days early but billing cycle is shorter than 7 days","expected_behavior":"For billing cycles shorter than 7 days (e.g., weekly plans), the 7-day lead time exceeds the entire cycle. The system must define behavior — likely renew immediately or at cycle start."},{"scenario":"Customer creates trial, cancels it, then requests a new trial with the same email","expected_behavior":"BR-6 says 'active trial' — a cancelled trial should not block a new one. But this could enable serial trial abuse. Product must clarify whether lifetime or concurrent uniqueness is intended."},{"scenario":"3rd payment failure and 14-day grace period conflict for a subscriber who upgrades to Premium mid-retry","expected_behavior":"Spec is ambiguous. Does the Premium grace period apply retroactively to already-accumulated failures? Needs product decision."},{"scenario":"All payment methods on file are failed, not just the primary","expected_behavior":"BR-1 says 'a failed payment method on file' — unclear whether ANY failed method blocks activation or only the designated billing method. Likely intent is the billing method specifically."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"These rules directly govern revenue collection. The 7-day early renewal maximizes successful payment capture. The 3-strike and 14-day grace rules balance revenue protection against involuntary churn. Trial uniqueness prevents revenue leakage from free-tier abuse.","operational":"Auto-cancellation reduces manual intervention for delinquent accounts. The Premium grace period adds complexity to the cancellation pipeline — two distinct paths must be maintained. Trial deduplication requires reliable email identity resolution.","user":"Customers benefit from retry windows (not immediately cancelled on first failure). Premium subscribers receive preferential treatment, reinforcing the value proposition of the premium tier. The no-reactivation rule may frustrate customers who expect to 'resume' — clear UX messaging is critical.","risk":"The terminal cancellation rule (BR-4) is high-risk for customer satisfaction if not communicated clearly. The trial uniqueness rule can be circumvented with email aliases (+tags, dots in Gmail) unless the system normalizes email addresses.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Subscribers (Standard)","impact":"Subject to 3-strike cancellation; cannot reactivate — must re-subscribe, potentially losing accumulated benefits or pricing."},{"group":"Subscribers (Premium)","impact":"Receive more lenient 14-day grace period, reinforcing premium value but creating expectation of preferential treatment."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue Team","impact":"Auto-cancellation and retry logic directly affect MRR, churn metrics, and revenue forecasting accuracy."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will handle escalations from customers who cannot reactivate cancelled subscriptions or who are blocked from creating trials."},{"group":"Product/Growth Team","impact":"Trial uniqueness constraint affects acquisition funnels; too strict may reduce signups, too loose enables abuse."}]},"confidence":{"level":72,"reasoning":"The spec is relatively clear and internally consistent for a product-level description. The six rules are well-defined with testable boundaries. Confidence is not higher because several operational details are unspecified: retry timing within the 7-day window, behavior on tier changes mid-grace-period, email normalization for trial dedup, and whether 'failed payment method' means any method or the designated billing method.","high_confidence_areas":["BR-1: Activation gate on payment method status is unambiguous","BR-4: Terminal cancellation state is clearly stated","BR-6: One-trial-per-email uniqueness is clearly stated","BR-3 vs BR-5: The distinction between standard (3-strike) and premium (14-day grace) is explicit"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether 'failed payment method on file' means any method or specifically the billing method","Retry cadence within the 7-day renewal window (how are the 3 attempts spaced?)","Behavior when a subscriber changes tier during an active grace period or retry sequence","Whether trial uniqueness is concurrent-only or lifetime per email","How billing cycles shorter than 7 days interact with the early-renewal rule"],"assumptions":["The 14-day grace period for Premium replaces the 3-strike rule entirely, not supplements it","'Failed payment method' refers to the payment method designated for billing, not all methods on file","Trial uniqueness is enforced on concurrent active trials, not lifetime","Cancellation from grace period expiry or 3-strike rule follows the same terminal state as any other cancellation (BR-4 applies)"]},"escalations":[],"rejection_reasons":[]}
